# Review: `deepmind/345.lean` â€” ErdÅ‘s Problem 345

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line): lines 1â€“15 match verbatim.
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): line 17 `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line (lines 19â€“26).
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# ErdÅ‘s Problem 345`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/345`.
- [ ] **Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem.** The module docstring contains only the heading, reference link, and a bibliography entry for [ErGr80]. A brief plain-English summary of the problem (e.g., "asks whether the threshold of completeness for k-th power sequences is eventually non-monotone") is missing from the module docstring. The problem description does appear in the theorem docstring (lines 47â€“58), but the checklist requires it in the module docstring as well.
- [x] Optional bibliography entries: includes `[ErGr80]` citation. âœ“

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos345 ... end Erdos345` (lines 30, 65).
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase`: `Erdos345`.
- [x] `open` statement appears **before** the namespace block: line 28 `open Finset BigOperators`.

## 5. Custom Definitions

- [x] `subsetSums` (line 34) has a LaTeX docstring (lines 32â€“33).
- [x] `thresholdOfCompleteness` (line 40) has a LaTeX docstring (lines 37â€“39) and is marked `noncomputable`.
- [x] `kthPowers` (line 44) has a LaTeX docstring (line 43).
- [x] No `sorry` in any definition.
- [x] Functions returning `Set â„•` / `â„•` use `lowerCamelCase`: `subsetSums`, `thresholdOfCompleteness`, `kthPowers`.
- [x] No local notation needed; none used.
- N/A: `@[category test]` / `@[category API]` sanity checks â€” optional, not present.

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword (line 60).
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_345` (snake_case, matches namespace number).
- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line** (line 59).
- [x] Has a docstring (lines 47â€“58) with a detailed description of the problem.
- [x] Docstring references the source paper: `[ErGr80, p.55]`.

### `answer()` usage

The problem asks "Is it true that there are infinitely many k such that T(n^k) > T(n^{k+1})?" â€” this is a **yes/no question** (Case 1).

- [x] Theorem type is `answer(sorry) â†” P` where `P` formalizes the question as asked.
- [x] The RHS formalizes the question faithfully: `Set.Infinite {k : â„• | thresholdOfCompleteness (kthPowers k) > thresholdOfCompleteness (kthPowers (k + 1))}`.
- [x] `answer(sorry)` is used correctly (problem is open, answer unknown).
- [x] Quantifiers are embedded in the RHS (via `Set.Infinite`), not as theorem parameters.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[category ...]`: `category research open` (line 59).
- [x] Value is valid: `research open`.
- [x] The `research open` theorem contains `sorry` in its proof body.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute: `AMS 5 11` (line 59).
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed (two: 5, 11).
- [x] Numbers are in ascending order: 5 < 11.
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] Both are valid AMS MSC2020 codes (5 = Combinatorics, 11 = Number theory). Appropriate for this problem about combinatorial number theory.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Uses acceptable multi-line `by` + `sorry` form (lines 62â€“63):
  ```lean
  ... := by
    sorry
  ```
- [x] No `sorry` in any `def`, `abbrev`, or `structure` declaration.
- [x] No informal proof sketches for the open research problem.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- N/A â€” no variants present. Single-part problem; no variants needed.

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `â„•`, `âˆ€`, `âˆƒ`, `âŠ†`, `âˆ§`, `âˆˆ`, `â‰¥`, `â†”`, `âˆ‘`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` that disables linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` â€” not verified in this review.
- [ ] Custom linter passes â€” not verified in this review.

---

## Summary

**Overall: PASS with one minor issue.**

The file is well-structured and conforms to nearly all checklist items. The formalization is mathematically faithful: `subsetSums` correctly models finite subset sums P(A), `thresholdOfCompleteness` captures T(A) as the infimum, `kthPowers k` gives {n^k : n â‰¥ 1}, and the theorem correctly uses `answer(sorry) â†” P` for the yes/no question. All naming conventions, attributes, and docstrings are properly handled.

**One issue to address:**

1. **Module docstring missing plain-English description (Â§3):** The module docstring (lines 19â€“26) has the heading, reference link, and bibliography but lacks a brief plain-English description of the problem. Adding a sentence such as _"Asks whether the threshold of completeness T (the least m such that all n â‰¥ m are representable as a finite subset sum) satisfies T(n^k) > T(n^{k+1}) for infinitely many k."_ would satisfy this requirement.

    22â†’*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/345](https://www.erdosproblems.com/345)
    23â†’
    24â†’[ErGr80] ErdÅ‘s, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number

    33â†’$P(A) = \{\sum_{n \in B} n : B \subseteq A, B \text{ finite}\}$. -/

    35â†’  {s | âˆƒ (B : Finset â„•), (â†‘B : Set â„•) âŠ† A âˆ§ s = âˆ‘ n âˆˆ B, n}
    36â†’
    37â†’/-- The threshold of completeness $T(A)$: the least $m$ such that
    38â†’all $n \ge m$ are in $P(A)$, the set of finite subset sums of $A$.
    39â†’(Only meaningful for complete sequences.) -/
    40â†’noncomputable def thresholdOfCompleteness (A : Set â„•) : â„• :=
    41â†’  sInf {m : â„• | âˆ€ n : â„•, n â‰¥ m â†’ n âˆˆ subsetSums A}
    42â†’
    43â†’/-- The set of $k$-th powers of positive integers: $\{1^k, 2^k, 3^k, \ldots\}$. -/

    45â†’  {m | âˆƒ n : â„•, n â‰¥ 1 âˆ§ m = n ^ k}
    46â†’

    48â†’ErdÅ‘s Problem 345 [ErGr80, p.55]:
    49â†’

file.content:
/-
Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

    https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0

Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
limitations under the License.
-/

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# ErdÅ‘s Problem 345

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/345](https://www.erdosproblems.com/345)

[ErGr80] ErdÅ‘s, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
theory_. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
-/

open Finset BigOperators

namespace Erdos345

/-- The set of all finite subset sums of a set $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$. That is,
$P(A) = \{\sum_{n \in B} n : B \subseteq A, B \text{ finite}\}$. -/
def subsetSums (A : Set â„•) : Set â„• :=
  {s | âˆƒ (B : Finset â„•), (â†‘B : Set â„•) âŠ† A âˆ§ s = âˆ‘ n âˆˆ B, n}

/-- The threshold of completeness $T(A)$: the least $m$ such that
all $n \ge m$ are in $P(A)$, the set of finite subset sums of $A$.
(Only meaningful for complete sequences.) -/
noncomputable def thresholdOfCompleteness (A : Set â„•) : â„• :=
  sInf {m : â„• | âˆ€ n : â„•, n â‰¥ m â†’ n âˆˆ subsetSums A}

/-- The set of $k$-th powers of positive integers: $\{1^k, 2^k, 3^k, \ldots\}$. -/
def kthPowers (k : â„•) : Set â„• :=
  {m | âˆƒ n : â„•, n â‰¥ 1 âˆ§ m = n ^ k}

/--
ErdÅ‘s Problem 345 [ErGr80, p.55]:

Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be a complete sequence, and define the threshold of completeness
$T(A)$ to be the least integer $m$ such that all $n \ge m$ are in
$P(A) = \{\sum_{n \in B} n : B \subseteq A, B \text{ finite}\}$.

Is it true that there are infinitely many $k$ such that $T(n^k) > T(n^{k+1})$?

Known values: $T(n) = 1$, $T(n^2) = 128$, $T(n^3) = 12758$, $T(n^4) = 5134240$,
and $T(n^5) = 67898771$.
-/
@[category research open, AMS 5 11]
theorem erdos_345 :
    answer(sorry) â†” Set.Infinite {k : â„• | thresholdOfCompleteness (kthPowers k) >
      thresholdOfCompleteness (kthPowers (k + 1))} := by
  sorry

end Erdos345

representable as a finite subset sum) satisfies $T(n^k) > T(n^{k+1})$ for infinitely many $k$.

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/345](https://www.erdosproblems.com/345)

end Erdos345

content.text: Added a plain-English description to the module docstring after the heading, as required by the checklist (Â§3). The description summarizes the problem: whether the threshold of completeness satisfies T(n^k) > T(n^{k+1}) for infinitely many k.
